site.name: Third Eye Worldwide
site.title: Third Eye Worldwide — Technology that opens new worlds

seo.defaultDescription: Third Eye Worldwide builds free, open-source voice-first assistive technology for blind and low-vision users — led by a visually impaired founder. A world beyond vision.

/.title: Third Eye Worldwide — Technology that opens new worlds
/.description: A world beyond vision. Free, open-source voice-first assistive technology for the 2.2 billion people worldwide with visual impairment — led by a visually impaired founder.

/about.title: About — Third Eye Worldwide
/about.description: An NGO building tools with — not just for — visually impaired people. Meet the team, the mission, and the values that guide every product decision.

/projects.title: Projects — Third Eye Worldwide
/projects.description: Voice-first tools that let blind and low-vision users operate the web and each other's stories — no sighted assumptions. Two projects, one mission.

/donate.title: Donate — Third Eye Worldwide
/donate.description: Every dollar funds free tools, free training, and free devices for visually impaired people worldwide. 94¢ of every dollar goes to programs.

/media.title: Media — Third Eye Worldwide
/media.description: Photos, podcasts, and videos from the field — every item captioned, transcribed, and audio-described.

/documents.title: Documents — Third Eye Worldwide
/documents.description: Blogs, stories, and research from the TEWW team. Field studies, policy analysis, and community voices.

/volunteers.title: Volunteer — Third Eye Worldwide
/volunteers.description: Translators, developers, audio narrators, and community organisers always welcome. Every role is remote-friendly and paired with a mentor.

/coming-soon.title: Coming soon — Third Eye Worldwide
/coming-soon.description: Something new is launching soon at Third Eye Worldwide.

home.coreValuesSub: Every tool we ship, every partnership we sign, and every hire we make is measured against these. They are non-negotiable.

coreValues.title: Dignity first
coreValues.desc: Accessibility is not a feature we add at the end — it is the foundation we start from. Every user deserves full, independent access with no compromise.

coreValues.title: Our founder is visually impaired
coreValues.desc: No product decision gets made without lived experience in the room. Our founder lives the problem we build for — and steers every design call accordingly.

coreValues.title: Open by default
coreValues.desc: All our core software is MIT-licensed. Translations are crowd-sourced. Knowledge and access should never be gatekept by cost, language, or geography.

coreValues.title: Built for where people live
coreValues.desc: We design for 2G networks, five-year-old smartphones, and the last mile. If it only works in a lab in San Francisco, it doesn't work.

coreValues.title: Radical transparency
coreValues.desc: Every dollar donated is tracked to a program. Every impact number is auditable. Every mistake we make is published — because trust is earned, not claimed.

features.title: Screen Reader
features.desc: Real-time audio descriptions and voice navigation for any web or mobile interface — in 40+ languages.

features.title: Visual Magnifier
features.desc: Dynamic screen magnification with edge detection and color enhancement for low-vision users.

features.title: Navigation Aid
features.desc: Audio-described wayfinding that guides users through cities, transit systems, and indoor spaces.

features.title: Digital Literacy
features.desc: Training workshops that teach visually impaired individuals to use technology confidently and safely.

features.title: Community Access
features.desc: Local partnerships that distribute devices and data plans to underserved blind and low-vision communities.

features.title: Open Source
features.desc: All core tools are MIT licensed. Developers worldwide contribute fixes, translations, and features.

projects.heroSub: Voice-first tools that let blind and low-vision users operate the web and each other's stories without a single sighted assumption.

items.title: TE — AI Voice Navigator
items.desc: Say "Hey TE" and go anywhere on our site, read the page aloud, switch themes, or control the audio tour — no mouse, no keyboard.
items.what: TE (Third Eye) is our voice-first navigator. It listens for the wake phrase "Hey TE", takes a spoken command, performs the action — navigate, read aloud, control the audio tour, switch theme — and confirms verbally. TE is built into every page on our website.
items.how: A small client-side agent uses the browser's Web Speech API to listen continuously for the wake word. When it fires, a second recognition pass captures the command, which is routed through a rules-based intent parser. Confirmations are spoken back via speech synthesis. Commands map to keyword patterns — there is no cloud round-trip and no network dependency once the page has loaded, so TE keeps working on slow or intermittent connections.
items.why: Mouse and keyboard navigation assume a user can see the interface. For someone blind or low-vision, every click is a search. TE removes the search: speak the destination and get there. It also helps hands-free users — caregivers, users with motor impairments — explore the same site with no pointing device at all.
items.usage: Click the TE pill under the top navigation and grant microphone permission once. After that, say "Hey TE" anywhere on the site. Try: "Hey TE, go to donate." "Hey TE, read this page." "Hey TE, dark mode." "Hey TE, pause the audio." Say "Hey TE, help" for the full command list, or "goodbye" to turn TE off.
items.future: Today TE only lives in our web browser. Next milestones: a standalone iOS and Android app so TE travels with our users, then a browser extension that brings the same voice interface to any site on the internet, and finally an OS-level integration that runs TE alongside system screen readers without fighting them for focus.

items.title: Third Eye World
items.desc: A voice-first social network built from scratch for blind and low-vision users. Every post is a voice memo. Every action is a voice command.
items.what: Third Eye World is a social platform built exclusively for blind and low-vision users. Posts are voice memos — no images, no text, no visual-first timelines. Navigation is voice-driven; interactions are deliberately simple: like and comment, nothing else. Accounts exist, but there is no follow graph, no ranking algorithm, and no vanity metrics.
items.how: The whole app is organised around voice. Users record a voice memo to post. The timeline is an audio stream — memos play one after another. To interact, the user speaks a simple command ("like", "comment", "next") or taps a single on-screen button that the screen reader announces clearly. Comments are themselves voice memos. Server-side, memos are stored as short audio files with machine-generated transcripts for search — never displayed visually by default.
items.why: Mainstream social media was designed for sighted browsing: visual feeds, thumb-scroll mechanics, endless follow graphs. For blind users, adapting that through a screen reader is exhausting and slow. We wanted a platform that starts from voice as the first-class input and output — one where a blind user is the default user, not an accommodation.
items.usage: Create an account with your voice (TE guides the onboarding flow). Record a voice memo to post — up to two minutes. The timeline plays new memos from the community. Say "like" to like, "comment" to reply with your own short memo, or "next" to skip. There's no follow button and no algorithmic feed; everyone you follow in spirit is simply everyone on Third Eye World.
items.future: Third Eye World is in build. The roadmap: private beta with fifty blind testers in Nairobi, Lagos, and Dhaka; a voice transcription and search layer for memo discovery; accessibility certification (WCAG 2.2 AAA as a floor, then real-world field validation); moderation tooling that flags abusive audio without requiring visual review; and finally a global open beta paired with free onboarding training in every user's first language.

documents.heroSub: Long-form writing from our team and community — research, opinion, and the stories of people our work reaches.

blogs.title: Why 'accessibility mode' is the wrong mental model
blogs.desc: Bolting accessibility onto a product late is how we ended up with second-class experiences. We argue for voice-first as the default — not an afterthought mode.
blogs.author: Said Mohaddes Sadeqi

blogs.title: Building for 2G: performance lessons from the last mile
blogs.desc: Our first users live on networks where a 200KB page is a meaningful cost. Here's what we changed in the build pipeline — and why it helped sighted users too.
blogs.author: Said Mohaddes Sadeqi

blogs.title: What Pete Gustin taught us about voice-first interface design
blogs.desc: A blind longboard surfer can feel terrain his eyes can't report. We learned the same lesson building TE — the richest signals often aren't visual.
blogs.author: Sayed Towfiq Mowafaq

blogs.title: Field notes from our first three users
blogs.desc: Hours on real devices with real users who had never touched a screen reader. Patterns we did not expect, and three fixes we shipped the next week.
blogs.author: Tom DeMeo

stories.title: Pete Gustin and the feel of a wave
stories.desc: A longboard surfer who has been blind for most of his career reads waves with his feet, ears, and a spotter in the channel. His craft is a working demo of what 'voice-first' and 'feel-first' can be.
stories.author: TEWW Editorial

stories.title: Haben Girma — lawyer, author, first deafblind Harvard Law graduate
stories.desc: Haben's career is a reminder that the 'access' in accessibility is a practice, not a compliance checkbox. She changed the courts. She is still changing the internet.
stories.author: TEWW Editorial

stories.title: Erik Weihenmayer's Everest — and the quiet engineering behind it
stories.desc: The first blind climber to summit Everest did it with a team, a rope, and a patiently-built toolkit of haptic and verbal cues. Good assistive tech is the same discipline at smaller scale.
stories.author: TEWW Editorial

stories.title: Christine Ha cooks without sight — and teaches why that matters
stories.desc: The MasterChef winner runs a restaurant kitchen by smell, sound, timing, and muscle memory. Every visit is a case study in adaptive design, translated into the web.
stories.author: TEWW Editorial

featuredStory.pullQuote: I don't miss the wave. I feel it coming.
featuredStory.title: Pete Gustin and the feel of a wave
featuredStory.desc: Pete Gustin has been a professional longboard surfer for most of his adult life — and blind for most of that. His craft is built on touch, sound, timing, and a trusted spotter. We follow his line to understand why voice-first tools we build for the web borrow the same principle: the richest signals often aren't visual.
featuredStory.author: TEWW Editorial

volunteers.heroSub: Our work happens because 800+ volunteers around the world give what they can — an hour, an afternoon, a pull request. Here's where to start.

roles.title: Translator
roles.desc: Localise our interfaces, docs, and audio scripts into your language.

roles.title: Open-source contributor
roles.desc: Write and review code for our Screen Reader, Magnifier, and Navigation apps.

roles.title: Audio narrator
roles.desc: Record human voice-overs for our Audiolibrary. Every voice counts.

roles.title: Training facilitator
roles.desc: Lead our 4-week digital literacy bootcamps, in-person or virtual.

roles.title: Accessibility tester
roles.desc: Help test new releases. Guided sessions — no prior testing experience needed.

roles.title: Community organiser
roles.desc: Build a local TEWW chapter. Host events, workshops, and recruitment.

roles.title: Writer & editor
roles.desc: Contribute to our blog, stories, and grant applications.

roles.title: Photographer / videographer
roles.desc: Document field work — stories, events, training days. With consent.

steps.title: Apply
steps.desc: Fill out a short form. It takes 6 minutes. We read every application within 48 hours.

steps.title: Orientation call
steps.desc: A 30-minute video or phone call with a program coordinator to match you to the right role.

steps.title: Onboarding
steps.desc: Self-paced training, plus a live kickoff session with other new volunteers in your region.

steps.title: Meet your mentor
steps.desc: Every volunteer is paired with an experienced mentor for their first three months.

steps.title: Start contributing
steps.desc: First task, first translation, first code review — whatever your role, you're shipping in week two.

about.heroSub: Five honest questions about Third Eye Worldwide — who we are, what we do, what we stand for, and why we think you should come build with us.

faqs.question: Who are we?
faqs.body:
Third Eye Worldwide (TEWW) is a registered non-profit technology NGO, founded in 2025 by a visually impaired engineer who believed the tools available to blind and low-vision users were decades behind what was possible.

Today we're a small team — three full-time staff and six active volunteers — building voice-first, open-source tools for the blind and low-vision community. Our founder lives the problem we build for: no product decision ships without lived experience in the room.

faqs.question: What do we do?
faqs.body:
We build voice-first, open-source assistive tools — free at point of use. Today we have two active projects:

  • TE — AI Voice Navigator. A voice assistant built into our website. Say "Hey TE" and go anywhere on the site, read the page aloud, switch themes, or control the audio tour — no mouse, no keyboard.
  • Third Eye World. A voice-first social network for blind and low-vision users — every post is a voice memo, every action is a voice command. In build; public beta coming soon.

Everything we ship is MIT-licensed forever, designed for the lowest-bandwidth conditions, and shaped by users who live with visual impairment.

faqs.question: Our mission.
faqs.body:
To give every blind and low-vision person on earth full, independent, dignified access to the digital world — at no cost, on whatever device they already own.

We measure the mission in three ways: the number of people who gain a capability they didn't have yesterday, the share of our tools that stay free and open forever, and the percentage of users who say our software feels like it was built for them — not adapted to them.

faqs.question: Our values?
faqs.body:
Five non-negotiables. Every hire, product decision, and partnership gets measured against them:

  • Dignity first. Accessibility isn't a feature we bolt on — it's the foundation we start from.
  • Nothing about us, without us. No product, policy, or program is designed without the people it serves in the room.
  • Open by default. MIT-licensed code, crowd-sourced translations, public research.
  • Built for where people live. If it only works in a San Francisco lab, it doesn't work.
  • Radical transparency. Every dollar is traceable, every impact number auditable, every mistake we make is published.

faqs.question: Why should you join us?
faqs.body:
Because the work is unambiguous, measurable, and personal. Every feature you ship, every language you translate, every voice command you tune lands in the hands of a real person who gains independence they didn't have before.

  • You'll have ownership. Tiny team, real mandates. We're three full-time staff and six volunteers; new contributors ship to production in their first week.
  • Founder-led accessibility. Our founder is visually impaired — every product decision is weighed against lived experience before it ships.
  • Your work stays free. Everything you build is MIT-licensed forever. No acquisitions, no paywalls, no "pro tier" someday.
  • Voice-first, by design. We're building tools where blind users are the default users, not an accommodation.

team.name: Said Mohaddes Sadeqi
team.bio: Engineer, educator, and humanitarian innovator dedicated to creating an inclusive world through technology and compassion.

team.name: Kenneth Lafond
team.bio: Computer engineer with 14 years at Intel. Founder of ADEvantage Technology; serves on the Advisory Board.

team.name: Tom DeMeo
team.bio: Ecologist with a 38-year US Forest Service career. Refugee mentor and volunteer with multiple humanitarian organizations.

team.name: Sayed Towfiq Mowafaq
team.bio: Humanitarian and former U.S. military interpreter from Afghanistan. Co-founder focused on expanding opportunities for the blind community.
